content:
6 min read 1 hour ago -- Press enter or click to view image in full size What if HTML stopped being the source of truth for web applications and instead became just one of several outputs generated from a richer semantic model? Introduction For over three decades, HTML has been the foundation of the Web. Every website, from a personal blog to Google’s search engine, is ultimately built upon HTML, styled with CSS, and made interactive with JavaScript. This combination has survived countless technological revolutions because it works remarkably well. But today’s web is no longer just a collection of hyperlinked documents. It has become an application platform. Modern web applications increasingly resemble desktop software, mobile apps, and even games. At the same time, new UI frameworks such as Flutter, Flet, Rio, Compose Multiplatform, SwiftUI, and various WebAssembly-based technologies are encouraging developers to think in terms of widgets, components, and declarative UI trees instead of manually writing HTML. Meanwhile, AI is fundamentally changing how software interacts with the Web. This raises an interesting question: What if HTML was no longer the primary representation of a web application? Not because HTML is obsolete — but because it may no longer be the best source of truth. The Web Has Changed When HTML was invented, the browser’s primary job was to display documents. Today’s browsers do far more: • Run complex applications • Execute games • Render GPU-accelerated graphics • Perform video editing • Host IDEs • Execute WebAssembly applications The browser has evolved from a document viewer into a rendering platform. Yet developers still spend enormous effort translating application logic into HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. HTML Is Excellent — but It Was Designed for Documents HTML excels at describing documents.

Introduction

...

However, applications often consist of concepts that HTML only approximates: • Dashboards • Trading terminals • CAD software • Game interfaces • Complex design tools • Visual programming environments Developers frequently resort to deeply nested
elements because HTML lacks higher-level concepts for many application interfaces. Ironically, frameworks already solve this problem internally. Flutter has Widgets. SwiftUI has Views. Jetpack Compose has Composables. Flet has Controls. React has Components. The developer already works with an abstract UI model long before HTML exists. Pixel-Perfect UI Frameworks Frameworks such as Flutter and Flet increasingly render interfaces independently of traditional HTML layouts. Instead of thinking in terms of HTML elements, developers describe the interface declaratively: ft.Column( controls=[ ft.Text("Python Trading"), ft.ElevatedButton("Enroll") ] )Internally, this is a semantic description of the interface. Eventually it becomes pixels on the screen. Humans do not see HTML. Humans see rendered pixels. The Hidden Problem with Today’s Web Ironically, humans are no longer the only consumers of websites. Today, websites are also consumed by: • Search engines • AI assistants • Autonomous agents • Screen readers • Browser automation • Testing frameworks • Translation systems Each of these consumers wants something different. Humans want beautiful rendering. Screen readers want semantics. Google wants structured information. AI wants meaningful context. Testing tools want stable UI relationships. Today we attempt to satisfy all of them using HTML. That creates unnecessary complexity. Vision AI Reveals an Interesting Truth Recent research into multi-modal retrieval systems (often called Vision RAG) demonstrates that many semantic relationships are lost when complex webpages are reduced to plain text. Tables lose structure. Charts lose geometry. Dashboards lose spatial relationships. Visual hierarchy disappears. A rendered webpage often contains information that cannot be faithfully reconstructed from extracted text alone. Vision models understand webpages much like humans do: by looking at the rendered result. This does not mean HTML is obsolete. It means HTML is no longer the only meaningful representation. The Real Source of Truth Should Be a Semantic UI Tree Instead of making HTML the canonical representation, imagine making the application’s semantic UI model the primary artifact. Application Code │ ▼ Semantic UI TreeThis semantic tree describes the interface at a conceptual level. For example: Page: title: Python Trading CourseNavigation: - Home - Courses - ContactHeading: level: 1 text: Python Trading CoursePrimaryButton: text: Enroll NowDataTable: columns: - Lesson - DurationNotice that this representation contains meaning rather than presentation. Everything Else Becomes an Output Once the Semantic UI Tree exists, multiple outputs become possible. Semantic UI Tree │ ┌────────────────┼────────────────┐ │ │ │ ▼ ▼ ▼ HTML Accessibility Render Tree │ │ │ ▼ ▼ ▼ Google Screen Readers GPU Renderer ┌────────────────────┐ ▼ ▼ AI Context Testing APIsInstead of writing HTML directly, HTML becomes one possible compilation target. Other outputs include: • Accessibility Trees • Structured data • AI-friendly semantic representations • Automated testing metadata • Localization resources • Printable documents • Native mobile interfaces One source. Many consumers. Why This Is Better Than OCR One might ask: “Why not simply render the application and let AI recover the semantics from the pixels?” Because that throws away information the framework already knows. If a framework creates: Button( text="Delete Account", destructive=True )it already knows: • It is a button. • It is destructive. • It requires confirmation. • It triggers a specific action. Rendering it into pixels and then asking AI to rediscover those facts is unnecessary. AI should enrich semantics — not reconstruct them. Where AI Actually Helps AI still has an important role. Suppose a framework creates several generic containers. The framework may not know they collectively represent navigation. AI can infer: “These components form the site’s primary navigation.” The resulting HTML becomes:
instead of
Similarly, AI can improve: • Landmark detection • Heading hierarchy • Accessibility descriptions • Metadata generation • SEO enhancements • Semantic labeling AI becomes an assistant for semantics rather than a replacement for them. Accessibility Improves Naturally Accessibility should never depend on OCR. Screen readers do not care about pixels. They care about concepts. They want to know: • Heading • Navigation • Button • Checkbox • Table • Form • Alert A Semantic UI Tree provides these concepts directly. No reverse engineering required. SEO Also Benefits Search engines care about semantics far more than presentation. They want to understand: • Titles • Articles • Products • Breadcrumbs • Authors • Reviews • FAQs Generating semantic HTML from a Semantic UI Tree is likely more reliable than asking developers to manually maintain it. The rendered interface remains pixel-perfect. Search engines receive optimized semantic information. Both audiences are satisfied. A Browser API for Semantic Trees Today’s browsers internally maintain multiple representations: • DOM • CSSOM • Accessibility Tree • Render Tree • Compositing Layers What if browsers standardized another one? Semantic UI Tree APIApplications could expose semantics directly to: • Search engines • Accessibility software • AI agents • Automation frameworks without forcing every consumer to reconstruct meaning from HTML. HTML would remain fully supported. It would simply become one serialization among many. A Practical Architecture Python / Rust / C# │ ▼ Declarative UI Framework (Flet, Rio, Flutter, ...) │ ▼ Semantic UI Tree │ ┌───────────────┼────────────────┐ │ │ │ ▼ ▼ ▼ Canvas/WebGPU HTML Accessibility │ │ │ ▼ ▼ ▼ Human Users Search Screen Readers ┌──────────────────────────────┐ ▼ ▼ AI Semantic Output Testing MetadataThe Semantic UI Tree becomes the single source of truth. Every consumer receives the representation best suited to its needs. Isn’t This Already Happening? To some extent, yes. The industry is slowly moving toward higher-level UI abstractions. Developers increasingly write: • React Components • Flutter Widgets • SwiftUI Views • Compose Composable • Flet Controls rather than raw HTML. The missing piece is treating that semantic representation as a first-class artifact rather than merely an intermediate step. Challenges This vision is not without obstacles. Some important questions remain: • How should semantic trees be standardized? • How should interactive state be synchronized? • How should browser extensions interact with semantic APIs? • How should crawlers validate generated semantics? • How should frameworks expose custom widgets? These are engineering challenges — not conceptual impossibilities. Conclusion This proposal is not about replacing HTML. HTML transformed the world and will remain fundamental for years to come. Instead, it proposes a shift in perspective. The primary representation of a modern web application should no longer be markup. It should be meaning. From that meaning, we can generate: • Pixel-perfect rendering for humans. • Semantic HTML for search engines. • Accessibility trees for assistive technologies. • Rich representations for AI agents. • Stable metadata for testing. • Structured outputs for future platforms. In the early days of computing, developers wrote assembly language directly. Today, we write higher-level languages that compile into assembly. Perhaps HTML is approaching a similar evolution. Not disappearing. But becoming a compilation target generated from something richer, more expressive, and more aligned with how modern applications are actually built. The web began as a network of documents. It has become a platform for applications. Maybe it’s time for its semantic model to evolve accordingly. … What do you think? Please share your thoughts and experiences on this matter.
